questions.answer: A. Output is "221" and 2^n - 1 characters are printed
questions.to_find: Output when abc("12") is called, and number of characters printed for string of length n

understand.plain: The function abc is recursive. For a non-empty string, it first calls itself twice on s+1 (the string shifted by one), and only after both calls return does it print the first character s[0]. This means printing happens in post-order, and each character triggers two sub-calls before being printed.

keywords.explain: The print statement comes after all recursive calls, so characters are printed on the way back up the recursion tree.
keywords.example: abc("1") calls abc("2") twice then prints '1'

keywords.explain: For abc(s) on a string of length n, the tree has depth n with two children at each internal node, giving 2^n - 1 internal nodes where printing occurs.
keywords.example: abc("12"): 1 root + 2 nodes for '2' = 3 prints total = 2^2 - 1

understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree for abc("12"): root calls abc("2") twice, each abc("2") calls abc("") twice (base case returns), then prints '2'. After both abc("2") return, root prints '1'. Output: 221.

given.aim: Determine the output of abc("12") and derive a formula for the number of characters printed for a string of length n.

terms.meaning: Recursive function that calls abc(s+1) twice before printing s[0]
terms.example: abc("A") prints 'A' after two returns from abc("")
terms.connects: Post-order traversal means deeper characters print before shallower ones

terms.term: T(n) = characters printed for string of length n

terms.meaning: Characters are printed only after both recursive subcalls complete
terms.example: '1' prints last because abc("2") is called twice first
terms.connects: Determines the order of output: rightmost first, then root

given.plan: Trace abc("12") manually using the recursion tree to find the output order. Then set up the recurrence T(n) = 2*T(n-1) + 1 with T(0)=0 and solve.

steps.title: Trace abc("12")
steps.apply:
abc("12"): calls abc("2") [first], abc("2") [second], then prints '1'.
abc("2") [first]: calls abc("") [base, returns], abc("") [base, returns], then prints '2'.
abc("2") [second]: calls abc("") [base, returns], abc("") [base, returns], then prints '2'.
Print sequence: '2' (from first abc("2")), '2' (from second abc("2")), '1' (from abc("12")).
Output = "221"
steps.note: The post-order placement means inner (deeper) characters print before outer ones.

steps.title: Derive number of characters printed for string of length n
steps.apply:
Each call on a string of length n makes 2 recursive calls on a string of length n-1, then prints 1 character.
T(0) = 0 (empty string, just returns)
T(1) = 2*T(0) + 1 = 1
T(2) = 2*T(1) + 1 = 3
T(n) = 2^n - 1
Verify: T(2) = 2^2 - 1 = 3 (printed '2','2','1') correct.
steps.note: This is a standard geometric series: T(n) = 2^n - 1.

solution.result: Output of abc("12") is "221"; for a string of length n, 2^n - 1 characters are printed. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Post-order recursion with branching factor 2; recurrence T(n) = 2*T(n-1) + 1 solves to T(n) = 2^n - 1.

questions.answer: Condition: n > 0; First move call: (n-1, A, C, B); Second move call: (n-1, B, A, C)
questions.to_find: Fill in the three blanks in the Towers of Hanoi recursive function

understand.plain: The Towers of Hanoi problem asks us to move n disks from pole A to pole C using pole B as auxiliary. The recursive strategy is: move the top n-1 disks from A to B (using C as temp), then move the largest disk directly from A to C, then move the n-1 disks from B to C (using A as temp).

keywords.explain: Classic recursion problem: move n disks from source to destination using an auxiliary peg, following the rule that a larger disk cannot be placed on a smaller one.
keywords.example: For n=1: just move disk 1 from A to C directly.

keywords.explain: Break moving n disks into: move n-1 disks out of the way, move the largest disk, move n-1 disks back.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 460 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='200' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>move(n, A, B, C)</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='130' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='69' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Step 1:</text><text x='85' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>move(n-1, A, C, B)</text><rect x='165' y='50' width='130' height='44' rx='8' class='ok'/><text x='230' y='69' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>Step 2:</text><text x='230' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>print A -> C</text><rect x='310' y='50' width='130' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='375' y='69' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Step 3:</text><text x='375' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>move(n-1, B, A, C)</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub mut'>Move n-1 disks</text><text x='20' y='148' class='sub mut'>from A to B</text><text x='20' y='166' class='sub mut'>(using C as aux)</text><text x='165' y='130' class='sub mut'>Move disk n</text><text x='165' y='148' class='sub mut'>from A to C</text><text x='310' y='130' class='sub mut'>Move n-1 disks</text><text x='310' y='148' class='sub mut'>from B to C</text><text x='310' y='166' class='sub mut'>(using A as aux)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-step Towers of Hanoi strategy: (1) move n-1 disks from A to B using C, (2) move disk n from A to C directly, (3) move n-1 disks from B to C using A.

given.aim: Fill in the three blank parts of the recursive Towers of Hanoi function.

terms.meaning: Move n disks from pole A to pole C using pole B as auxiliary
terms.example: move(1, A, B, C) just prints 'Move disk 1 from A to C'
terms.connects: Defines what the function does; the recursive calls swap the roles of B and C

terms.meaning: Only move disks when n is at least 1; if n=0, do nothing
terms.example: if(n > 0) guards the recursive calls and the print statement

terms.meaning: First sub-call: A is source, B is destination, C is auxiliary -> (n-1, A, C, B). Second sub-call: B is source, C is destination, A is auxiliary -> (n-1, B, A, C)
terms.example: The roles of B and C are swapped to route disks correctly
terms.connects: Ensures the largest disk can be placed on C without violating constraints

given.plan: Identify the classic Towers of Hanoi logic: condition is n>0; first recursive call moves n-1 disks from A to B (using C as auxiliary), so arguments are (n-1, A, C, B); second recursive call moves n-1 disks from B to C (using A as auxiliary), so arguments are (n-1, B, A, C).

steps.title: Determine the base condition
steps.apply: The function should do nothing when there are no disks to move (n=0). So the condition is n > 0. The if-body runs only for n >= 1, making n=0 the implicit base case.
steps.note: Equivalently, some implementations use n >= 1 as the condition.

steps.title: Fill in the first recursive call
steps.apply: Before moving disk n from A to C, we must clear the top n-1 disks out of the way to pole B. Pole C serves as the temporary peg during this step. So the call is move(n-1, A, C, B): move n-1 disks from A to B using C as helper.
steps.note: Notice: the destination B and auxiliary C swap compared to the outer call signature move(n, A, B, C).

steps.title: Fill in the second recursive call
steps.apply: After printing (moving disk n from A to C), the n-1 disks are on pole B and must go to pole C. Pole A is now free to serve as auxiliary. So the call is move(n-1, B, A, C): move n-1 disks from B to C using A as helper.
steps.note: Complete solution: if(n>0){ move(n-1,A,C,B); print; move(n-1,B,A,C); }

solution.result: Blank 1 (condition): n > 0. Blank 2 (first call): (n-1, A, C, B). Blank 3 (second call): (n-1, B, A, C).

questions.formula_note: Classic Towers of Hanoi recursive decomposition: move n-1 disks to auxiliary, move largest disk to destination, move n-1 disks from auxiliary to destination. Requires 2^n - 1 moves total.

questions.to_find: The value returned by f(1) where i is a static variable initialized to 1

understand.plain: The function f uses a static variable i that persists across all recursive calls (it is NOT reset on each call). Starting with n=1, each call adds i to n and increments i, then calls f again recursively until n >= 5. Because i is static, it keeps increasing across successive recursive calls.

keywords.explain: A local variable declared static retains its value between function calls. It is initialized only once at program start.
keywords.example: static int i = 1 means i starts at 1 and persists; after i++ it becomes 2 for the next call.

keywords.explain: Each recursive invocation of f shares the same static i, so i increases monotonically across all calls, not resetting to 1 each time.
keywords.example: Call 1: i=1; Call 2: i=2; Call 3: i=3

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 460 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='220' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Trace of f(1) with static i</text><rect x='10' y='44' width='440' height='34' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='66' class='sub ink'>Call 1: n=1, i=1  n=1+1=2, i becomes 2  call f(2)</text><rect x='10' y='84' width='440' height='34' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='106' class='sub ink'>Call 2: n=2, i=2  n=2+2=4, i becomes 3  call f(4)</text><rect x='10' y='124' width='440' height='34' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='146' class='sub ink'>Call 3: n=4, i=3  n=4+3=7, i becomes 4  call f(7)</text><rect x='10' y='164' width='440' height='34' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='20' y='186' class='sub' style='fill:white'>Call 4: n=7  7 >= 5  return 7</text><text x='230' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f(1) returns 7</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Trace table: Call 1 n=1 i=1 gives n=2 i=2; Call 2 n=2 i=2 gives n=4 i=3; Call 3 n=4 i=3 gives n=7 i=4; Call 4 n=7 >= 5 returns 7.

given.aim: Trace f(1) step by step with the static variable i to find the final returned value.

terms.meaning: Current value tested and modified in each call
terms.connects: When n >= 5, it is returned as the final answer

terms.meaning: Initialized to 1 once; incremented each call and shared across all recursive calls
terms.example: i=1 in call 1, i=2 in call 2, i=3 in call 3
terms.connects: Added to n each call; grows cumulatively affecting the result

terms.meaning: When n reaches or exceeds 5, return n immediately without more recursion
terms.connects: Terminates recursion and produces the final answer

given.plan: Simulate each call step by step, carefully tracking both n (which changes per call) and static i (which persists across all calls).

steps.title: Simulate all recursive calls
steps.formula_raw: n = n + i; i++; return f(n);
steps.apply:
Call 1: f(1): n=1, i=1; 1 < 5; n = 1+1 = 2; i becomes 2; tail-call f(2)
Call 2: f(2): n=2, i=2; 2 < 5; n = 2+2 = 4; i becomes 3; tail-call f(4)
Call 3: f(4): n=4, i=3; 4 < 5; n = 4+3 = 7; i becomes 4; tail-call f(7)
Call 4: f(7): n=7, i=4; 7 >= 5; return 7
steps.note: Key insight: i is static so its value carries forward from call to call without resetting to 1.

steps.title: Propagate the return value
steps.apply: f(7) returns 7 to f(4), which returns 7 to f(2), which returns 7 to f(1). Final answer = 7.
steps.note: Each intermediate call simply returns whatever f(n) returns, so 7 propagates all the way back to the original caller.

questions.formula_note: Static variable behavior in C: initialized once and retains its value across all function calls; unlike regular local variables which are re-initialized on each call.

questions.to_find: Space complexity of the memoized version of foo()

understand.plain: The original foo(n) recomputes foo(0), foo(1), ..., foo(n-1) repeatedly (exponential time). If we memoize by storing each computed value foo(i) the first time we compute it, we only need to store n values (for i=0 to n-1). Storing n values requires O(n) extra space.

keywords.explain: Storing the results of expensive function calls so that repeated calls with the same arguments return the cached result instead of recomputing.
keywords.example: Store foo(0)=1, foo(1)=1, foo(2)=2, ... in an array; each value computed only once.

keywords.explain: The amount of extra memory required by an algorithm, typically expressed in Big-O notation.
keywords.example: Storing an array of n values requires O(n) space.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 460 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='460' height='180' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Memoization table for foo()</text><rect x='30' y='44' width='60' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>memo[0]</text><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>1.0</text><rect x='100' y='44' width='60' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>memo[1]</text><text x='130' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>1.0</text><rect x='170' y='44' width='60' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>memo[2]</text><text x='200' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>2.0</text><rect x='240' y='44' width='60' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>memo[3]</text><text x='270' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>4.0</text><rect x='310' y='44' width='80' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>... up to</text><text x='350' y='84' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>memo[n-1]</text><text x='230' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Array of n entries  =  O(n) space</text><text x='230' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Space Complexity: O(n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memo array storing foo(0)=1, foo(1)=1, foo(2)=2, foo(3)=4, ..., foo(n-1). Total n entries require O(n) space.

given.aim: Determine the space complexity of the memoized foo() function.

terms.meaning: foo(n) = sum of foo(i) for i=0 to n-1, with foo(0)=1.0
terms.connects: Without memoization, this causes exponential recomputation

terms.meaning: An array memo[0..n-1] that stores foo(i) once computed, avoiding recomputation
terms.example: After computing foo(3)=4, store memo[3]=4 for reuse
terms.connects: The size of this table determines the extra space complexity

terms.meaning: foo(0), foo(1), ..., foo(n-1) - exactly n distinct values to store
terms.example: For foo(5), store 5 values: foo(0) through foo(4)

given.plan: Count how many distinct values need to be stored in the memoization table: exactly n values for foo(0) through foo(n-1). This gives O(n) space.

steps.title: Identify values computed by foo(n)
steps.apply: To compute foo(n), we need foo(0) through foo(n-1). These are n distinct values. Without memoization, each foo(k) gets recomputed many times (exponential). With memoization, each of the n values is computed exactly once and stored.
steps.note: The original foo has O(2^n) time complexity due to repeated subproblems.

steps.title: Determine storage required
steps.formula_raw: Space = O(n) for memo array of size n
steps.apply: We need to store foo(0), foo(1), ..., foo(n-1) — exactly n double values. This requires a memo array of size n, which uses O(n) extra space. The recursion stack depth is O(n), also O(n). Total extra space = O(n).
steps.note: Storing n values is O(n) regardless of the constant factor (size of each double).

solution.result: Space complexity of memoized foo() is O(n). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Memoization stores one result per unique subproblem; foo(n) has n distinct subproblems foo(0) through foo(n-1), requiring O(n) extra space.
